did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes a melanoma detection system that uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) for image preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, and classification of skin lesions. It reports an accuracy of 95.24% for the CNN-based system and 93.48% for the DNN model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original melanoma detection system using CNNs and DNNs, detailing a proposed architecture, implementation workflow (image acquisition, preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification), and specific experimental results (95.24% accuracy for CNN, 93.48% for DNN). It is published in the 2025 International Conference on Computing and Communication Technologies (ICCCT) and describes new technical contributions rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: MELANOMA CANCER DETECTION SYSTEM
journal: 2025 International Conference on Computing and Communication Technologies (ICCCT)
brief_summary: This study presents a melanoma detection system utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant. The pipeline incorporates image preprocessing, segmentation using Otsu's thresholding and Active Contour Models, and feature extraction via Discrete Cosine Transform and Histogram of Oriented Gradients. The system is designed for interoperability with Electronic Medical Records and telemedicine platforms to support remote diagnosis. Evaluation results indicate that the CNN-based model achieved an accuracy of 95.24%, while the DNN model reached 93.48%. The authors conclude that the system offers a robust, efficient solution for early melanoma detection with potential for future integration of multimodal data and Vision Transformers.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the detection system; identified as a life-threatening form of skin cancer originating from melanocytes. The system aims to distinguish melanoma from benign lesions.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The system categorizes lesions as either benign or malignant (melanoma). Benign lesions are described as typically having smooth borders and single color, contrasting with malignant ones.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The system performs binary classification to categorize skin lesions as either benign or malignant (melanoma). The primary models evaluated are a CNN-based system (95.24% accuracy) and a DNN model (93.48% accuracy). The output includes a classification result and a confidence score (probability) representing the degree of categorization confidence.

datasets_used.name: Not specified
datasets_used.description: The paper describes a proposed melanoma detection system using CNNs and DNNs but does not explicitly name the specific dataset, cohort, or image collection used for training or testing. It references the HAM10000 dataset in the bibliography as a comprehensive dataset of pigmented skin lesions, but does not state that it was used in this study's experiments.

use.other: The system was evaluated to achieve 95.24% accuracy (CNN) and 93.48% accuracy (DNN), but the source of the data for these results is not specified in the text.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

Not specified.notes: The paper does not explicitly name the specific dataset used for these results. It mentions that the CNN-based system delivers 95.24% accuracy, better computational efficiency, and reduced complexity. The DNN model, combined with a random feature coordination schema, achieved 93.48% accuracy, outperforming conventional methods like SVM and basic neural networks.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Deep Neural Network (DNN)

cnn_layers: Convolutional Layers (for feature extraction like edges and textures)
Pooling Layers (for dimensionality reduction and computational efficiency)
Fully Connected Layers (for combining features and classification)
feature_extraction_methods: Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) for color variation
Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) for border irregularity
Fractal Dimension analysis for border irregularity
Color histograms in RGB and HSV spaces

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected Layers with probability distribution
classification_or_output_head.output: Binary classification (benign vs. malignant) with confidence score

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian Smoothing (applied to filter out high-frequency noise while preserving edges)
Histogram Equalization (applied for contrast enhancement by redistributing pixel intensity values)
Normalization (applied to maintain uniformity in brightness levels)
Resizing (applied to maintain uniformity in image dimensions)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as a method used to solve dataset imbalance in the context of the related work 'Melanoma Detection Leveraging Advanced Deep Learning Models' (which used over 36,000 skin lesion photos from SIIM and ISIC datasets), but no specific techniques, probabilities, or settings are reported for the primary system proposed in this paper.

conclusions: The proposed melanoma detection system, leveraging Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), achieved an accuracy of 95.24% for the CNN-based model and 93.48% for the DNN model.
The CNN-based system demonstrated better computational efficiency and reduced complexity compared to other methods.
The DNN model, when combined with a random feature coordination schema, outperformed conventional classification methods such as Simple Vector Machines (SVM) and basic neural networks.
The system is designed to be interoperable with Electronic Medical Records (EMR) systems and telemedicine platforms, facilitating data-driven clinical decision-making and remote diagnosis.
The system aims to alleviate the workload of healthcare professionals, enhance diagnostic precision, and make dermatological care more accessible, particularly in resource-limited areas.

Future work includes integrating Vision Transformers (ViTs), expanding datasets to include diverse skin tones, incorporating multimodal data (genetic, clinical, imaging), and improving system explainability and regulatory compliance to foster broader clinical adoption.
important_remarks: The specific dataset used for training and testing the proposed CNN and DNN models is not explicitly named or described in the text, despite reporting accuracy metrics (95.24% for CNN, 93.48% for DNN).
Critical implementation details such as the optimizer, loss function, hyperparameters, and training schedule are not reported.
The paper does not report standard deviation, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests for the reported accuracy results.
There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about generalizability and potential overfitting to the unspecified data source.
The paper does not specify the demographic diversity of the dataset, particularly regarding skin tones, which is a known bias risk in melanoma detection systems.
Data leakage risks cannot be assessed due to the lack of detail on train/validation/test splits and data augmentation specifics for the primary system.

The system's clinical relevance is currently limited to a proof-of-concept stage, with future work explicitly identifying the need for diverse datasets, multimodal data integration, and regulatory compliance before broader clinical adoption.
